intro:
Wisdom in the OT (Hebrew chokmah) encompasses practical expertise (the skill of craftsmen — Ex. 28:3; 31:3; experienced governance — 1 Kings 3:28; 4:29-34; moral discernment in daily life) and theological orientation (the recognition that 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' — Prov. 1:7; 9:10; Ps. 111:10; Job 28:28). These two dimensions are inseparable in biblical thought: wisdom is skill in living rightly, and living rightly is impossible without right orientation toward God. The personification of Wisdom as a woman who calls from the streets and city gates (Prov. 1:20-33; 8:1-9:6) represents wisdom not merely as an abstract quality but as an active divine agent offering life and understanding to all who will listen.

Proverbs 8:22-31 develops the personification further, describing Wisdom as present with God at creation: 'I was there when he set the heavens in place... I was the craftsman at his side' (8:27-30). This became the primary OT resource for NT Christological reflection: Colossians 1:15-17 identifies Christ as 'the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation... all things have been created through him and for him'; John 1:1-3 identifies the eternal Word with the agent of creation; Hebrews 1:2-3 describes the Son as the one 'through whom he made the universe' and 'the radiance of God's glory.' The Wisdom tradition and the Logos tradition converge in NT Christology to affirm that the personal agent through whom God created the world was the same one who became incarnate in Jesus Christ — 'the wisdom of God' made visible (1 Cor. 1:24, 30; Col. 2:3).
